turns.content: I'm trying to build a CLI parser in Rust but I keep getting confused by the manual parsing setup. Can anyone explain how to use #[derive(Parser)] instead of writing custom parsers?

Using #[derive(Parser)] is much simpler than manual parsing because it automatically generates a trait implementation for your command structure. You just need to define an enum like my_cli::Command with variants for each subcommand, and then derive Parser on that enum. This lets the crate infer argument definitions from field names without you manually matching regex patterns or building complex parsers.
That sounds great, but I thought every command would automatically generate help output if I just used derive. Why does my program not show the help message when I run it with --help?

The issue is that #[derive(Parser)] alone doesn't enable help generation by default; you need to explicitly call .enable_help(true) on your Parser instance. Also, make sure the version string in Cargo.toml matches what clap expects so it can be extracted correctly from the manifest.

I just tried to build a simple CLI with clap using #[derive(Parser)] and it works, but I noticed my help output is missing the version string even though I defined it in Cargo.toml. Should I manually set up the Args struct or add extra attributes?

You don't need manual setup if you're already using the derive macro correctly; clap will automatically pull the version from your Cargo.toml metadata. The common mistake here is forgetting to use the feature flag in Cargo.toml, so ensure you have [dependencies] with clap = "4" and then add the optional-features line for help-json or other extras if needed, but for basic versioning it just needs the standard derive call on your Args struct without extra manual configuration.

Okay, I added that to Cargo.toml and now the version shows up. But wait, I'm using a Subcommand enum with #[derive(Parser)] on each variant separately, which seems wrong since clap expects it on the parent type. Can't I just derive it once on the enum?

You cannot derive Parser directly on individual subcommand variants because clap requires all arguments to be grouped under a single parent struct that holds the Enum itself; you must define a main Args struct, then use #[clap(subcommand)] to attach the enum inside it. Deriving on each variant separately is the mistake beginners make when they think clap applies per-variant rather than per-container type.

Ah, I see now, so if I have an Enum with variants like Command::Login and Command::Logout, I need a separate struct holding that enum? But what if I want different argument groups for each command? Can't I just put them inside the same Args struct without extra wrappers?

Yes, you can keep everything in one struct, but to have distinct argument groups per subcommand, you should use #[clap(subcommand)] on your enum field within the main Args struct and then define separate structs for each variant like LoginArgs and LogoutArgs that get associated with those variants. This way clap treats them as separate argument parsers internally while still being part of one unified command structure.
